answers:
**1. Where should new refund provider calls live, and does the code already follow that pattern?**

New refund calls belong in `medibill/backend/app/billing/gateway.py:1-17`, following the existing `charge()`/`create_customer()` accessor pattern (e.g. a new `refund(...)` function that calls `_client.refund(...)` on the module-level `_client`). This is exactly what `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md:5-7` prescribes: "Every call to the payment provider goes through app/billing/gateway.py; the only exception is app/billing/errors.py."

The code does **not** follow that pattern everywhere today. I found two places that talk to the provider a different way, both bypassing `gateway.py` entirely by importing `chargekit` directly and constructing their own `chargekit.Client`:

- `medibill/backend/app/api/invoices.py:4` and `:14` — `charge_invoice()` does `import chargekit` then `client = chargekit.Client(api_key_env="CHARGEKIT_API_KEY")` and calls `client.charge(...)` directly, rather than calling `gateway.charge()`. This has been present since the very first commit, `23d2f37` ("MEDB-180 initial billing backend and architecture doc") — the *same commit* that wrote the "everything goes through gateway.py" rule into the architecture doc. So this violation is as old as the doc itself.
- `medibill/backend/app/workers/dunning.py:2` and `:9` — `run_dunning_pass()` similarly does `import chargekit` and instantiates `chargekit.Client(api_key_env="CHARGEKIT_API_KEY")` inline, then calls `.charge(...)`. This was added in commit `3a7daee` ("MEDB-197 nightly dunning retry worker") and also never referenced or reconciled against the architecture doc.

`medibill/backend/app/billing/errors.py:6` (`from chargekit import errors as ck_errors`) is the one documented, legitimate exception, and it correctly imports only the exception classes, not a client.

So as things stand there are two undocumented violations of the "single boundary module" rule (`invoices.py`, `dunning.py`) in addition to the one documented, legitimate exception (`errors.py`). Grepping confirms these are the only three files touching `chargekit` outside `gateway.py`: `grep -rn "chargekit" ./medibill` returns matches only in `errors.py`, `gateway.py`, `dunning.py`, `invoices.py`, and a prose mention in `README.md:4`.

**2. `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` vs. actual code — invoice API response and payment integration.**

- **Payment integration boundary claim** (`docs/ARCHITECTURE.md:5-7`): "Every call to the payment provider goes through app/billing/gateway.py; the only exception is app/billing/errors.py." As shown in answer 1, this is false today: `backend/app/api/invoices.py` and `backend/app/workers/dunning.py` also call the provider directly, and the doc lists neither as an exception.
- **Invoice API response claim** (`docs/ARCHITECTURE.md:11-12`): "The Invoice API response contains exactly the fields: id, tenant_id, amount_cents, currency, status" (5 fields). The actual `InvoiceOut` dataclass in `backend/app/models/invoice.py:16-22` has six fields: `id, tenant_id, amount_cents, currency, status, external_ref`. This drift was introduced in commit `c23da88` ("MEDB-214 record external payment ref on invoices"), which added `external_ref` to both `Invoice` and `InvoiceOut` and wired it into `InvoiceOut.from_model()` (`invoice.py:24-28`) and into `charge_invoice()` (`invoices.py:16-18`), and added a matching column via migration `medibill/backend/migrations/0006_add_invoice_external_ref.sql` ("ALTER TABLE invoice ADD COLUMN external_ref TEXT"), but did not touch `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` in that commit or any later one.

**3. What should happen to `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md`, and when?**

Per the guiding skill, this doc is meant to be treated as merge-blocking law with drift treated as a bug, updated in the same commit as the code that changes what it governs — not left to go stale. Concretely, before any refund feature work lands, the doc needs three corrections, and they should have already happened in the commits that caused the drift (retroactively, they should happen now, before new provider code is added on top of an already-inaccurate contract):

- Update the "Payment integration" section to either (a) fix `invoices.py` and `dunning.py` to route through `gateway.py`, making the doc true again, or (b) if there's a real reason those two call sites can't go through `gateway.py`, add them to an explicit, honest exception list next to `errors.py` (the skill's "Enforce a module dependency direction, and name the exceptions" guidance). Given there's no apparent reason `charge()` in `gateway.py` couldn't serve both call sites, the correct fix is almost certainly (a): make `invoices.py` and `dunning.py` call `gateway.charge()` instead of instantiating their own client, restoring the doc-as-written to being true.
- Update the "Invoice API" section to list all six current fields, including `external_ref`.
- This should happen now, before the refund feature is designed — the new refund endpoint will add itself as a *third* call site if the existing pattern is copied uncritically, and any refund-related invoice/webhook shape decisions should be made against an accurate baseline, not a doc that's already been wrong since commit `23d2f37`/silently drifted further at `c23da88`. When the refund work itself lands, its own provider call must go through `gateway.py`, and any change to the invoice response or webhook set it introduces must update `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` in that same commit, per the skill's core rule.

**4. Webhook event types claim.**

`docs/ARCHITECTURE.md:16-17` states: "The integration handles 4 webhook event types: payment_succeeded, payment_failed, refund_created, dispute_opened." The actual handler in `medibill/backend/app/api/webhooks.py:9-20` checks five event kinds: `payment_succeeded`, `payment_failed`, `payment_pending`, `refund_created`, `dispute_opened` — `payment_pending` is handled but not mentioned in the doc. This was added in commit `c23da88` ("MEDB-214 record external payment ref on invoices"), the same commit that added `external_ref`, again without a corresponding doc update.

What I'd change: update the sentence to "5 webhook event types" and add `payment_pending` to the list. More importantly, per the skill's "Don't cite a number: read the source" guidance, a hardcoded count like "4" (or "5") is guaranteed to drift again the next time a handler is added — as it already has once. I'd consider dropping the literal count and instead either (a) enumerating the list without a count ("the integration handles the following webhook event types: ..."), or (b) pointing at the source of truth ("see the `if kind == ...` branches in `app/api/webhooks.py` for the current list") so the number never has to be kept in sync by hand. Given the refund feature is likely to add further webhook types (e.g. a `refund_failed` event, if chargekit emits one), a self-maintaining phrasing here specifically avoids this doc drifting a second time.
report:
## Plan

I set out to determine, for a future refund feature, where payment-provider calls should live, and to check whether `medibill/docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` still matches the actual backend code — specifically the invoice API response shape, the "single boundary module" invariant, and the claimed webhook event types. I first read the guiding skill (`architecture-contracts-as-law`), which frames the architecture doc as a merge-blocking contract that must be updated in the same commit as any code that touches what it governs, and treats drift as a bug. I then located the repo that actually has this domain (payment/invoicing) and read every file that touches the provider SDK, plus the git history to see when drift was introduced.

## Actions

1. Read `.claude/skills/architecture-contracts-as-law/SKILL.md` (full file) — this is the guide referenced in the task; it establishes that architecture-contract docs are law, drift is a bug, vendor SDK calls must be funneled through one module with an explicit, current exception list, and violations should be greppable.
2. `find . -maxdepth 3` from the working root — the workspace contains many unrelated sibling repos (carelog-api, cedarline, clinic-comms, etc.); `medibill` is the one with a billing backend, `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md`, and `tests/test_webhooks.py`, matching the payment/invoice/refund domain in the task.
3. `find ./medibill -not -path '*/.git*' -type f` — enumerated every file in the medibill repo.
4. Read `medibill/docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` — states the provider-call boundary rule, the exact invoice response fields, and "4 webhook event types."
5. Read `medibill/backend/app/billing/gateway.py` — the documented single boundary module; builds the `chargekit.Client` once and exposes `charge()` and `create_customer()` accessors.
6. Read `medibill/backend/app/billing/errors.py` — the documented exception, imports only `chargekit.errors` exception classes.
7. Read `medibill/backend/app/api/invoices.py` — imports `chargekit` directly and constructs its own `chargekit.Client(...)` inside `charge_invoice`, instead of calling `gateway.charge()`.
8. Read `medibill/backend/app/api/webhooks.py` — handles `payment_succeeded`, `payment_failed`, `payment_pending`, `refund_created`, `dispute_opened` (5 branches).
9. Read `medibill/backend/app/models/invoice.py` — `InvoiceOut` dataclass has 6 fields: `id, tenant_id, amount_cents, currency, status, external_ref`.
10. Read `medibill/backend/app/workers/dunning.py` — imports `chargekit` directly and constructs its own `chargekit.Client(...)` inside `run_dunning_pass`, again bypassing `gateway.py`.
11. `grep -rn "chargekit" ./medibill` — confirmed the complete set of files that reference the SDK: `errors.py`, `gateway.py`, `dunning.py`, `invoices.py`, and a mention in `README.md`. No other file in the repo imports `chargekit`.
12. Read `medibill/tests/test_webhooks.py` — only a placeholder test (`assert True`); no coverage of webhook branches or the boundary rule.
13. Read `medibill/README.md` — points to `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` as the source of truth for the provider integration.
14. `git log --stat --oneline` and `git show <hash>` for all three commits in medibill's history (`23d2f37`, `3a7daee`, `c23da88`) — established when each piece of drift was introduced and confirmed no commit touched `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` after the initial one.
15. Read `medibill/frontend/src/App.jsx` and `medibill/infra/main.tf` — both placeholder stubs, no provider-related code.
16. `cat backend/migrations/*.sql` — confirmed the `invoice` table schema, including the `external_ref` column added in migration `0006`.
17. `git log --oneline --all` / `git branch -a` — confirmed a single `main` branch with exactly these three commits; no other branches hiding relevant changes.

## Answers

**1. Where should new refund provider calls live, and does the code already follow that pattern?**

New refund calls belong in `medibill/backend/app/billing/gateway.py:1-17`, following the existing `charge()`/`create_customer()` accessor pattern (e.g. a new `refund(...)` function that calls `_client.refund(...)` on the module-level `_client`). This is exactly what `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md:5-7` prescribes: "Every call to the payment provider goes through app/billing/gateway.py; the only exception is app/billing/errors.py."

The code does **not** follow that pattern everywhere today. I found two places that talk to the provider a different way, both bypassing `gateway.py` entirely by importing `chargekit` directly and constructing their own `chargekit.Client`:

- `medibill/backend/app/api/invoices.py:4` and `:14` — `charge_invoice()` does `import chargekit` then `client = chargekit.Client(api_key_env="CHARGEKIT_API_KEY")` and calls `client.charge(...)` directly, rather than calling `gateway.charge()`. This has been present since the very first commit, `23d2f37` ("MEDB-180 initial billing backend and architecture doc") — the *same commit* that wrote the "everything goes through gateway.py" rule into the architecture doc. So this violation is as old as the doc itself.
- `medibill/backend/app/workers/dunning.py:2` and `:9` — `run_dunning_pass()` similarly does `import chargekit` and instantiates `chargekit.Client(api_key_env="CHARGEKIT_API_KEY")` inline, then calls `.charge(...)`. This was added in commit `3a7daee` ("MEDB-197 nightly dunning retry worker") and also never referenced or reconciled against the architecture doc.

`medibill/backend/app/billing/errors.py:6` (`from chargekit import errors as ck_errors`) is the one documented, legitimate exception, and it correctly imports only the exception classes, not a client.

So as things stand there are two undocumented violations of the "single boundary module" rule (`invoices.py`, `dunning.py`) in addition to the one documented, legitimate exception (`errors.py`). Grepping confirms these are the only three files touching `chargekit` outside `gateway.py`: `grep -rn "chargekit" ./medibill` returns matches only in `errors.py`, `gateway.py`, `dunning.py`, `invoices.py`, and a prose mention in `README.md:4`.

**2. `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` vs. actual code — invoice API response and payment integration.**

- **Payment integration boundary claim** (`docs/ARCHITECTURE.md:5-7`): "Every call to the payment provider goes through app/billing/gateway.py; the only exception is app/billing/errors.py." As shown in answer 1, this is false today: `backend/app/api/invoices.py` and `backend/app/workers/dunning.py` also call the provider directly, and the doc lists neither as an exception.
- **Invoice API response claim** (`docs/ARCHITECTURE.md:11-12`): "The Invoice API response contains exactly the fields: id, tenant_id, amount_cents, currency, status" (5 fields). The actual `InvoiceOut` dataclass in `backend/app/models/invoice.py:16-22` has six fields: `id, tenant_id, amount_cents, currency, status, external_ref`. This drift was introduced in commit `c23da88` ("MEDB-214 record external payment ref on invoices"), which added `external_ref` to both `Invoice` and `InvoiceOut` and wired it into `InvoiceOut.from_model()` (`invoice.py:24-28`) and into `charge_invoice()` (`invoices.py:16-18`), and added a matching column via migration `medibill/backend/migrations/0006_add_invoice_external_ref.sql` ("ALTER TABLE invoice ADD COLUMN external_ref TEXT"), but did not touch `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` in that commit or any later one.

**3. What should happen to `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md`, and when?**

Per the guiding skill, this doc is meant to be treated as merge-blocking law with drift treated as a bug, updated in the same commit as the code that changes what it governs — not left to go stale. Concretely, before any refund feature work lands, the doc needs three corrections, and they should have already happened in the commits that caused the drift (retroactively, they should happen now, before new provider code is added on top of an already-inaccurate contract):

- Update the "Payment integration" section to either (a) fix `invoices.py` and `dunning.py` to route through `gateway.py`, making the doc true again, or (b) if there's a real reason those two call sites can't go through `gateway.py`, add them to an explicit, honest exception list next to `errors.py` (the skill's "Enforce a module dependency direction, and name the exceptions" guidance). Given there's no apparent reason `charge()` in `gateway.py` couldn't serve both call sites, the correct fix is almost certainly (a): make `invoices.py` and `dunning.py` call `gateway.charge()` instead of instantiating their own client, restoring the doc-as-written to being true.
- Update the "Invoice API" section to list all six current fields, including `external_ref`.
- This should happen now, before the refund feature is designed — the new refund endpoint will add itself as a *third* call site if the existing pattern is copied uncritically, and any refund-related invoice/webhook shape decisions should be made against an accurate baseline, not a doc that's already been wrong since commit `23d2f37`/silently drifted further at `c23da88`. When the refund work itself lands, its own provider call must go through `gateway.py`, and any change to the invoice response or webhook set it introduces must update `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` in that same commit, per the skill's core rule.

`docs/ARCHITECTURE.md:16-17` states: "The integration handles 4 webhook event types: payment_succeeded, payment_failed, refund_created, dispute_opened." More importantly, per the skill's "Don't cite a number: read the source" guidance, a hardcoded count like "4" (or "5") is guaranteed to drift again the next time a handler is added — as it already has once. I'd consider dropping the literal count and instead either (a) enumerating the list without a count ("the integration handles the following webhook event types: ..."), or (b) pointing at the source of truth ("see the `if kind == ...` branches in `app/api/webhooks.py` for the current list") so the number never has to be kept in sync by hand. Given the refund feature is likely to add further webhook types (e.g. a `refund_failed` event, if chargekit emits one), a self-maintaining phrasing here specifically avoids this doc drifting a second time.

## Next steps

If I were allowed to make changes, I would, in order:
1. Fix `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` now, in its own commit, to reflect current reality: name `invoices.py`/`dunning.py` as exceptions (or fix them), list all 6 invoice response fields, and list all 5 (not 4) webhook types — ideally rephrased to avoid a hardcoded count, per the skill.
2. Refactor `invoices.py` and `dunning.py` to call `gateway.charge()` instead of building their own `chargekit.Client`, so the "single boundary module" rule is actually true rather than aspirational — likely as a prerequisite change before adding refund code, so the refund feature has one clean pattern to follow rather than three inconsistent ones.
3. Add a `refund(tenant_id, amount_cents, currency="USD")` accessor to `gateway.py`, mirroring `charge()`/`create_customer()`, and have the new refund endpoint call it exclusively.
4. Extend `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md`'s webhook section for any new refund-related event(s) chargekit sends (e.g. `refund_failed`) in the same commit that adds the handler branch.
5. Add real test coverage for the webhook branches and the boundary rule itself — e.g. a grep-based guard test (as the skill suggests) asserting no file outside `gateway.py`/`errors.py`/(new) `refund` accessor imports `chargekit` directly, so this class of drift can't reappear silently. (I noticed `clinic-notes/scripts/check_tracked_calls.py` in a sibling repo appears to implement exactly this kind of guard script for its own vendor SDK boundary — worth looking at as a reference pattern, though I did not read it in detail since it's outside medibill.)

## Confidence and gaps

- High confidence on all four answers: each is grounded in a direct file read (with line numbers) and cross-checked against `git log`/`git show` for when the drift was introduced, plus a repo-wide `grep -rn "chargekit"` to confirm completeness of the "every place that talks to the provider" list. I'm confident I found all provider call sites in this repo — the grep found only 5 files, all read in full.
- One assumption: I inferred that `medibill` is the correct repo for this task from its content (billing backend, ARCHITECTURE.md, chargekit/payment domain) among many sibling repos in the workspace, since the task didn't name it explicitly. I did not exhaustively search every other sibling repo for a second "payment provider" integration — if another repo also touches a payment provider, I didn't find it, but the domain match here (invoices, dunning, webhooks, "payment provider" doc language) is strong enough that I'm confident this is the intended target.
- Gap: I don't know what refund-related methods the actual `chargekit` SDK exposes (e.g. exact method name/signature for a refund call, or what webhook event names it emits for refund failures) — the SDK itself isn't vendored in this repo (only imported as `import chargekit`, presumably a package dependency), so I could only reason from the existing `charge`/`create_customer` accessor shapes as a pattern, not confirm a `refund` method exists with a specific signature.
- Gap: `medibill/tests/test_webhooks.py` is a placeholder (`assert True`), so I could not cross-check the webhook event-type claim against test expectations — the only source of truth is the handler code itself, which I read directly.

Given there's no apparent reason `charge()` in `gateway.py` couldn't serve both call sites, the correct fix is almost certainly (a): make `invoices.py` and `dunning.py` call `gateway.charge()` instead of instantiating their own client, restoring the doc-as-written to being true.
- Update the "Invoice API" section to list all six current fields, including `external_ref`.
- This should happen now, before the refund feature is designed — the new refund endpoint will add itself as a *third* call site if the existing pattern is copied uncritically, and any refund-related invoice/webhook shape decisions should be made against an accurate baseline, not a doc that's already been wrong since commit `23d2f37`/silently drifted further at `c23da88`. When the refund work itself lands, its own provider call must go through `gateway.py`, and any change to the invoice response or webhook set it introduces must update `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` in that same commit, per the skill's core rule.

**4. Webhook event types claim.**

`docs/ARCHITECTURE.md:16-17` states: "The integration handles 4 webhook event types: payment_succeeded, payment_failed, refund_created, dispute_opened." The actual handler in `medibill/backend/app/api/webhooks.py:9-20` checks five event kinds: `payment_succeeded`, `payment_failed`, `payment_pending`, `refund_created`, `dispute_opened` — `payment_pending` is handled but not mentioned in the doc. This was added in commit `c23da88` ("MEDB-214 record external payment ref on invoices"), the same commit that added `external_ref`, again without a corresponding doc update.

What I'd change: update the sentence to "5 webhook event types" and add `payment_pending` to the list. More importantly, per the skill's "Don't cite a number: read the source" guidance, a hardcoded count like "4" (or "5") is guaranteed to drift again the next time a handler is added — as it already has once. I'd consider dropping the literal count and instead either (a) enumerating the list without a count ("the integration handles the following webhook event types: ..."), or (b) pointing at the source of truth ("see the `if kind == ...` branches in `app/api/webhooks.py` for the current list") so the number never has to be kept in sync by hand. Given the refund feature is likely to add further webhook types (e.g. a `refund_failed` event, if chargekit emits one), a self-maintaining phrasing here specifically avoids this doc drifting a second time.
sections.confidence and gaps:
- High confidence on all four answers: each is grounded in a direct file read (with line numbers) and cross-checked against `git log`/`git show` for when the drift was introduced, plus a repo-wide `grep -rn "chargekit"` to confirm completeness of the "every place that talks to the provider" list. I'm confident I found all provider call sites in this repo — the grep found only 5 files, all read in full.
- One assumption: I inferred that `medibill` is the correct repo for this task from its content (billing backend, ARCHITECTURE.md, chargekit/payment domain) among many sibling repos in the workspace, since the task didn't name it explicitly. I did not exhaustively search every other sibling repo for a second "payment provider" integration — if another repo also touches a payment provider, I didn't find it, but the domain match here (invoices, dunning, webhooks, "payment provider" doc language) is strong enough that I'm confident this is the intended target.
- Gap: I don't know what refund-related methods the actual `chargekit` SDK exposes (e.g. exact method name/signature for a refund call, or what webhook event names it emits for refund failures) — the SDK itself isn't vendored in this repo (only imported as `import chargekit`, presumably a package dependency), so I could only reason from the existing `charge`/`create_customer` accessor shapes as a pattern, not confirm a `refund` method exists with a specific signature.
- Gap: `medibill/tests/test_webhooks.py` is a placeholder (`assert True`), so I could not cross-check the webhook event-type claim against test expectations — the only source of truth is the handler code itself, which I read directly.
sections.next steps:
If I were allowed to make changes, I would, in order:
1. Fix `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` now, in its own commit, to reflect current reality: name `invoices.py`/`dunning.py` as exceptions (or fix them), list all 6 invoice response fields, and list all 5 (not 4) webhook types — ideally rephrased to avoid a hardcoded count, per the skill.
2. Refactor `invoices.py` and `dunning.py` to call `gateway.charge()` instead of building their own `chargekit.Client`, so the "single boundary module" rule is actually true rather than aspirational — likely as a prerequisite change before adding refund code, so the refund feature has one clean pattern to follow rather than three inconsistent ones.
3. Add a `refund(tenant_id, amount_cents, currency="USD")` accessor to `gateway.py`, mirroring `charge()`/`create_customer()`, and have the new refund endpoint call it exclusively.
4. Extend `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md`'s webhook section for any new refund-related event(s) chargekit sends (e.g. `refund_failed`) in the same commit that adds the handler branch.
5. Add real test coverage for the webhook branches and the boundary rule itself — e.g. a grep-based guard test (as the skill suggests) asserting no file outside `gateway.py`/`errors.py`/(new) `refund` accessor imports `chargekit` directly, so this class of drift can't reappear silently. (I noticed `clinic-notes/scripts/check_tracked_calls.py` in a sibling repo appears to implement exactly this kind of guard script for its own vendor SDK boundary — worth looking at as a reference pattern, though I did not read it in detail since it's outside medibill.)
sections.plan: I set out to determine, for a future refund feature, where payment-provider calls should live, and to check whether `medibill/docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` still matches the actual backend code — specifically the invoice API response shape, the "single boundary module" invariant, and the claimed webhook event types. I first read the guiding skill (`architecture-contracts-as-law`), which frames the architecture doc as a merge-blocking contract that must be updated in the same commit as any code that touches what it governs, and treats drift as a bug. I then located the repo that actually has this domain (payment/invoicing) and read every file that touches the provider SDK, plus the git history to see when drift was introduced.
